channel.title: Validated Patterns Workshop on Validated Patterns
channel.description: Recent content in Validated Patterns Workshop on Validated Patterns

item.title: Getting Started
item.description: Prerequisites The following CLI tools are required for running the exercises in this workshop. Please have them installed and configured prior to beginning any of the workshop chapters.

item.title: Introduction
item.description: What are Validated Patterns? Validated Patterns are an evolution of reference architectures. Each pattern is given a lifecycle when it is added to Red Hat’s continuous integration (CI) system because we’re testing against the latest version of operators, OpenShift releases and across multiple public cloud environments.

item.title: Demo
item.description: Validated Patterns Workshop Demo This interactive demo walks through installing the Validated Patterns operator and deploying the Multicloud Gitops pattern on a fresh OpenShift cluster using the Validated Patterns Catalog.

item.title: Pattern Landscape
item.description: Available Validated Patterns The number of available Patterns has significantly increased with more involvement from the field and our partners.

item.title: Partners
item.description: Working with Partners Partners have taken notice of what Validated Patterns can bring to their organizations. In turn, they have had focused development cycles to deploy their product/features using the Validated Patterns framework.

item.title: Pattern Tiers
item.description: Tiers The different tiers of Validated Patterns are designed to facilitate ongoing maintenance, support, and testing effort for a pattern.

item.title: Validated Patterns
item.description: Consuming Validated Patterns Let’s dive into what happens when we deploy a pattern! Take a look at the following graphic to see what is deployed on the hub clusterGroup cluster.

item.title: Values Files
item.description: Values Files A variety of values files are used in the Patterns framework - but the three most common you will come across will be:

item.title: Creating Patterns
item.description: Core Concepts Artifacts Artifacts can include Helm Charts, Kustomize manifests, or plain Kubernetes manifests Moving Artifacts into the Validated Patterns framework Many artifacts require conversion to Helm chart templates and parameterizing certain values

item.title: Patterns Operator
item.description: Validated Operator Features Available as a community operator in Operator Hub Interrogates the cluster and uses information to personalize the pattern with argo/helm values

item.title: Patterns Wrapper Script
item.description: Using the pattern.sh script $ ./pattern.sh 1 Welcome to the Validate Pattern utility container 111 It contains all the needed components to install a pattern.

item.title: To Fork or Not to Fork
item.description: Why fork? You need to be able to make changes for your own organization’s needs GitOps is all about git, merging changes safely is foundational Some changes make sense to be merged upstream You can make a pull request (PR) on the upstream pattern Typical recommended fork structure graph LR upstream["validatedpatterns/pattern-repo<br/>(upstream)"] -->|fork| origin["your-org/pattern-repo<br/>(origin)"] origin -->|clone| local["Local Workstation"] local -->|"git push"| origin origin -.

item.title: Secrets Management
item.description: Secrets Management vs. GitOps GitOps' entire premise is to use Git as the source of truth for infrastructure and application configuration

item.title: ESO and Vault
item.description: ESO and Vault The Validated Patterns team settled on using references to secrets We currently implement GitOps + Secrets via External Secrets Operator and HashiCorp Vault

item.title: Secrets Loading
item.description: Secrets Loading Loading secrets in the vault is usually done by running ./pattern.sh make load-secrets from the Pattern’s repository. This runs ansible code that parses local yaml files containing the secrets to be uploaded to the Vault running on the Hub

item.title: Self-Contained Patterns
item.description: Every Pattern has resources that make it unique: Container Images Containerfiles (aka: Dockerfiles) Helm Charts Kustomize Manifests Kubernetes Manifests

item.title: GitOps Rough Edges
item.description: Declarative Synchronization Making the transition to GitOps we’re often faced with the challenge of implementing applications and configurations that weren’t built with declarative deployments, more specifically, GitOps in mind

item.title: Sync Waves and Hooks
item.description: Sync waves are used in Argo CD to order how manifests are applied to the cluster. Resource hooks break up the delivery of these manifests into different phases.

item.title: Multiple Clusters
item.description: Why manage multiple clusters? Kubernetes adoption leads to multicluster "As Kubernetes gains adoption across the industry, scenarios are arising in which I&O teams are finding they must deploy and manage multiple clusters, either in a single region on-premises or in the cloud, or across multiple regions…​.

item.title: Additional Resources
item.description: Appendix - Additional Resources

item.title: Imperative Framework
item.description: Imperative vs. Declarative Actions What’s the difference between Imperative and Declarative actions? Declarative is when we describe what we want in our clusters, and we let the GitOps controller reconcile the difference between what’s in Git and what’s in the cluster.

item.title: Cluster Domains
item.description: Limitations By default ArgoCD does not expose any means to know the cluster’s name/FQDN (Fully Qualified Domain Name) To make up for this the framework collects some cluster information in order to provide reusable parameters for cluster specific values.

item.title: Multisource Patterns
item.description: What is MultiSource? Using multiple-sources in our ArgoCD applications gives the Patterns framework a lot of flexibility in how applications are deployed.
